subject: chemistry
title: Classifying Matter: Elements, Compounds, Mixtures
stem_template: Which of the following is a pure compound?
mark_scheme_template: Water is a pure compound with fixed composition (H:O = 2:1 by atoms). Air, brass, and crude oil are all mixtures with variable composition. [1 mark]
hints: A compound has fixed chemical formula and composition
Mixtures can be separated by physical means

subject: chemistry
title: States of Matter and Particle Model
stem_template: Which statement correctly describes particles in a liquid?
answer_template: Particles are close together but can move past each other
distractor_templates: Particles are fixed in regular lattice positions
Particles are widely spaced with negligible intermolecular forces
Particles move in straight lines only

options_template.text: Particles are close together but can move past each other
Particles are fixed in a regular lattice with only vibrational motion
Particles are widely spaced with negligible intermolecular forces
Particles move in straight lines only, never changing direction

mark_scheme_template: In liquids: particles close together (like solids) but have enough energy to flow past each other (unlike solids). Not widely spaced (that's gases). [1 mark]
hints: Compare with solids (ordered, fixed) and gases (random, sparse)

subject: chemistry
title: Separation Techniques
stem_template: Suggest a suitable separation technique for each mixture and explain the principle: (a) sand and water (b) ethanol and water (c) ink pigments in water.
mark_scheme_template: (a) Filtration — sand particles too large to pass through filter paper; water passes through [2]. (b) Fractional distillation — ethanol (bp 78°C) has lower boiling point than water (100°C); different volatilities [2]. (c) Paper/thin-layer chromatography — components have different solubilities in solvent/different affinity for stationary phase [2].
hints: Match technique to property difference
Consider particle size, boiling point, or solubility differences

subject: chemistry
title: Subatomic Particles
stem_template: An atom of \\(^{{A}}_{{{Z}}}\\)X has how many neutrons?
mark_scheme_template: Number of neutrons = mass number − atomic number = {{A}} − {{Z}} = {{A-Z}}. [1 mark]
hints: Neutrons = A − Z
A = mass number, Z = atomic number (= number of protons)

subject: chemistry
title: Isotopes
stem_template: Chlorine has two naturally occurring isotopes: \\(^{35}\\)Cl (abundance {{a35}}%) and \\(^{37}\\)Cl (abundance {{a37}}%). Calculate the relative atomic mass of chlorine.
hints: Ar = Σ(mass × abundance) / 100 for percentage abundances

subject: chemistry
title: Mass Spectrometry: Relative Atomic Mass from Spectrum
stem_template: A mass spectrum of element X shows peaks at m/z = {{m1}} (relative abundance {{a1}}) and m/z = {{m2}} (relative abundance {{a2}}). Calculate the relative atomic mass.
hints: Ar = Σ(mass × relative abundance) / Σ(relative abundance)
Divide by total abundance, not 100 (unless percentages)

subject: chemistry
title: Electron Configuration Notation
stem_template: What is the ground state electron configuration of iron (Z = 26)?
mark_scheme_template: Fe (Z=26): [Ar] = 18 electrons. Remaining 8: fill 3d then 4s. 3d can hold up to 10 electrons. Config: [Ar] 3d⁶ 4s² [1 mark]
hints: Aufbau principle: fill lowest energy orbitals first
Order: 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 4s 3d 4p...

subject: chemistry
title: Hund's Rule and Orbital Filling
stem_template: How many unpaired electrons does a nitrogen atom (Z = 7) have in its ground state?
mark_scheme_template: N: 1s² 2s² 2p³. The three 2p electrons occupy three separate p orbitals with parallel spins (Hund's rule), giving 3 unpaired electrons. [1 mark]
hints: Hund's rule: half-fill degenerate orbitals before pairing
N has 3 electrons in 2p: one in each px, py, pz

subject: chemistry
title: Electron Configuration of Transition Metal Ions
stem_template: Write the electron configurations of (a) Cu (Z=29) and (b) Cu²⁺, explaining any exceptions to the Aufbau principle.
mark_scheme_template: (a) Cu is [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹ (not [Ar] 3d⁹ 4s²) because a completely full d subshell gives extra stability [2]. (b) Cu²⁺: remove 2 electrons from the outermost shell (4s first, then 3d): [Ar] 3d⁹ [2].
hints: Cu and Cr are exceptions: half-full/full d subshells are extra stable
For ions: remove electrons from highest principal quantum number first

subject: chemistry
title: Molar Mass Calculation
stem_template: Calculate the molar mass of {{compound}} and hence find the number of moles in {{mass}} g.
mark_scheme_template: Molar masses: CaCO₃=100, H₂SO₄=98, NaOH=40, C₆H₁₂O₆=180, Fe₂O₃=160 g mol⁻¹. n = {{mass}} / M_r [2 marks]
hints: n = m / M_r
Add up all atomic masses in the formula

subject: chemistry
title: Empirical Formula from Percentage Composition
stem_template: A compound contains {{pC}}% carbon, {{pH}}% hydrogen, and the rest is oxygen. Determine its empirical formula. (Ar: C=12, H=1, O=16)
mark_scheme_template: %O = 100 − {{pC}} − {{pH}} = {{100-pC-pH}}%. Mole ratios: C: {{pC}}/12 = {{round(pC/12,3)}}; H: {{pH}}/1 = {{pH}}; O: {{100-pC-pH}}/16 = {{round((100-pC-pH)/16,3)}}. Divide by smallest and round to integers. [3 marks]
hints: Assume 100 g sample → % directly gives grams
Divide grams by molar mass to get moles
Divide all mole values by the smallest

subject: chemistry
title: Molecular Formula from Empirical Formula
stem_template: A compound has empirical formula CH₂O and molar mass 180 g mol⁻¹. Determine its molecular formula.
mark_scheme_template: Empirical formula mass: CH₂O = 12+2+16 = 30 g mol⁻¹ [1]. n = 180/30 = 6 [1]. Molecular formula = (CH₂O)₆ = C₆H₁₂O₆ [1].
hints: n = molar mass / empirical formula mass
Multiply subscripts in empirical formula by n

subject: chemistry
title: Ideal Gas Law Calculation
stem_template: Calculate the volume occupied by \\({{n}}\\) mol of an ideal gas at \\({{T}}\\) °C and \\({{P}}\\) kPa. (R = 8.31 J mol\\(^{-1}\\) K\\(^{-1}\\))
hints: pV = nRT
T in K = T(°C) + 273
P in Pa = P(kPa) × 1000

subject: chemistry
title: Molar Volume at STP
stem_template: At STP (0 °C, 100 kPa), what volume does 1 mole of any ideal gas occupy?
hints: IB uses STP = 0°C (273 K) and 100 kPa (not 101.325 kPa)

subject: chemistry
title: Ionic Bonding: Electron Transfer
stem_template: How many electrons does a {{element}} atom transfer to form its most stable ion?
mark_scheme_template: Magnesium: Group 2 → loses 2 electrons to achieve noble gas configuration → Mg²⁺ [1 mark]
hints: Group number indicates usual oxidation state/electrons lost/gained
Metals lose electrons, nonmetals gain electrons

subject: chemistry
title: Ionic Lattice Structure and Properties
stem_template: Explain why ionic compounds: (a) have high melting points, (b) conduct electricity when molten but not when solid, (c) are soluble in water.
answer_template: Strong electrostatic forces, mobile ions when molten, hydration by polar water
mark_scheme_template: (a) Strong electrostatic attractions between oppositely charged ions require large amounts of energy to overcome; giant ionic lattice [2]. (b) In solid: ions fixed in lattice, cannot move, so no current. In molten state: ions free to move and carry charge [2]. (c) Water molecules are polar; they hydrate ions by surrounding them with opposite partial charges, releasing lattice energy ≤ hydration energy [2].
hints: Think about ion mobility for conductivity
Hydration involves ion-dipole interactions

subject: chemistry
title: Predicting Ionic Formulae
stem_template: What is the correct formula for the ionic compound formed between calcium and phosphate (PO₄³⁻)?
mark_scheme_template: Ca²⁺ and PO₄³⁻: LCM of charges = 6. Need 3 Ca²⁺ and 2 PO₄³⁻: 3(+2) + 2(−3) = 0. Formula: Ca₃(PO₄)₂ [2 marks]
hints: Total positive charge must equal total negative charge
Find LCM of the charges

subject: chemistry
title: VSEPR Theory: Molecular Geometry
stem_template: Using VSEPR theory, predict the shape of a molecule with formula AX₃E₁ (3 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair around central atom).
mark_scheme_template: 4 electron pairs (3 bonding + 1 lone pair): based on tetrahedral electron geometry. Lone pair compresses bond angles → trigonal pyramidal shape. Example: NH₃, bond angle ≈ 107°. [1 mark]
hints: VSEPR counts all electron pairs around central atom
Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs

subject: chemistry
title: Lewis Structures and Resonance
stem_template: Draw the Lewis structure(s) of SO₂. Explain resonance and calculate the formal charge on the sulfur atom in each structure.
answer_template: Two resonance structures with S=O and S–O; formal charge varies
mark_scheme_template: Structure 1: S=O double bond on one side, S−O single bond with 3 lone pairs on the other; S has 1 lone pair [1]. Structure 2: mirror image [1]. Resonance: actual structure is average of both; S−O bonds intermediate length [1]. Formal charge on S in each structure: F.C. = valence e⁻ − lone pair e⁻ − ½ bonding e⁻ = 6 − 2 − ½(6) = +1 [2]. Modern view: S uses d orbitals, formal charge closer to 0.
hints: Count valence electrons first: S has 6, O has 6 each
Formal charge = V − LP − ½BP

subject: chemistry
title: Polarity of Molecules
stem_template: Which of the following molecules is non-polar despite having polar bonds?

options_template.text: HCl (diatomic — single bond dipole, polar molecule)

mark_scheme_template: CO₂ is linear (O=C=O); the two C=O bond dipoles are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, so they cancel to give a zero net dipole moment. [1 mark]
hints: Symmetrical molecules with identical bonds can be non-polar
Consider molecular shape, not just bond polarity

subject: chemistry
title: Metallic Bonding and Properties
stem_template: Which best explains why metals are good electrical conductors?
answer_template: Delocalized electrons can move freely through the metal lattice
distractor_templates: Metal ions vibrate to transfer energy
Metals contain free protons that carry charge
Ionic bonds in metals allow ion movement

options_template.text: Delocalized (free) electrons move freely through the metal lattice when a potential difference is applied
Metal ions vibrate and transfer kinetic energy along the lattice
Metals contain free protons that carry positive charge
Metal ions can move like in molten ionic compounds

mark_scheme_template: In metals: valence electrons leave individual atoms to form a 'sea' of delocalized electrons. Under applied voltage, these electrons drift in one direction → electric current. [1 mark]
hints: Delocalization is the key concept in metallic bonding

subject: chemistry
title: Alloys and Their Properties
stem_template: Explain why alloys are generally harder than pure metals. Use steel (iron + carbon) as an example.
answer_template: Different-sized atoms disrupt regular lattice → harder to deform
mark_scheme_template: Pure metal: regular lattice of identical atoms; layers can slide over each other (ductile, malleable) [1]. Alloy: atoms of different size disrupt regular pattern [1]; impurity atoms pin dislocation movement, preventing layer sliding [1]; makes alloy harder and less ductile [1]. Example: steel — carbon atoms fill interstitial spaces in Fe lattice, making it harder than pure iron [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Consider how layers of atoms slide past each other
Carbon atoms are smaller than iron atoms

subject: chemistry
title: Hydrogen Bonding
stem_template: Which pair of molecules forms hydrogen bonds between them?
distractor_templates: CH₄ and NH₃ — wait, NH₃ does form H-bonds

options_template.text: H\\(_2\\)O and CH\\(_3\\)OH (both have O−H groups)
CH\\(_4\\) and C\\(_2\\)H\\(_6\\) (only van der Waals forces)
CCl\\(_4\\) and CBr\\(_4\\) (only London dispersion forces)
HCl and HBr (only dipole-dipole forces, not H-bonds)

mark_scheme_template: Hydrogen bonding requires H bonded to highly electronegative atom (F, O, or N) AND a lone pair on F, O, or N in a neighboring molecule. H₂O and CH₃OH both have O−H bonds. [1 mark]
hints: H-bonds form only with N−H, O−H, or F−H groups

subject: chemistry
title: Trend in Boiling Points: Intermolecular Forces
stem_template: Arrange these molecules in order of increasing boiling point and explain: CH₄, NH₃, H₂O, HF. Discuss what types of intermolecular forces are present in each.
mark_scheme_template: CH₄ (−162°C): only London dispersion forces (small, non-polar) [1]. NH₃ (−33°C): N−H hydrogen bonding (but weaker network than H₂O due to only 1 H-bond donor/2 acceptors) [1]. HF (19°C): strong F−H hydrogen bonding (most electronegative F), but only 1 H per molecule limits H-bond network [1]. H₂O (100°C): highest — 2 H-bond donors AND 2 acceptors per molecule → extensive 3D hydrogen-bonding network [2]. [5 marks]
hints: Consider both the strength and number of H-bonds possible
London forces increase with molecule size

subject: chemistry
title: Periodic Trend: Ionization Energy
stem_template: Which explains why the first ionization energy of oxygen is lower than that of nitrogen?
answer_template: Oxygen's 2p⁴ configuration has a paired electron causing extra repulsion
distractor_templates: Oxygen has a larger atomic radius than nitrogen
Nitrogen has more protons attracting electrons more strongly
Oxygen is a noble gas with a full outer shell

options_template.text: Oxygen has a paired electron in 2p; electron-electron repulsion makes it easier to remove one electron
Oxygen has a larger atomic radius, so electrons are further from the nucleus
Nitrogen has more protons and attracts its electrons more strongly
Nitrogen has a completely half-filled 2p subshell which gives extra stability

mark_scheme_template: N: 2p³ (half-filled, one electron in each 2p orbital). O: 2p⁴ (one orbital has 2 electrons → repulsion). The extra repulsion in O's paired electron makes it easier to remove one → lower IE₁. [2 marks]
hints: Half-filled subshells (and full subshells) have extra stability
Paired electrons in same orbital repel each other

subject: chemistry
title: Electronegativity Trend
stem_template: Which element has the highest electronegativity?
mark_scheme_template: Fluorine has the highest electronegativity (Pauling: 3.98) because it has the most protons relative to shielding, smallest atomic radius, so strongest attraction for bonding electrons. [1 mark]
hints: Electronegativity increases across a period and up a group

subject: chemistry
title: Successive Ionization Energies and Group Identification
stem_template: The first five successive ionization energies (in kJ mol⁻¹) of element X are: 900, 1760, 14850, 21000, 33600. Identify the group of X and explain your reasoning.
mark_scheme_template: Large jump between 2nd and 3rd IE [1]. Indicates X has 2 electrons in its outer shell; removing the 3rd requires breaking into an inner shell [1]. Therefore X is in Group 2 [1]. Examples: Mg, Ca, etc. [1 mark for example]. [4 marks]
hints: Look for the large jump — it occurs when moving to a new shell
Number of electrons before the jump = group number

subject: chemistry
title: IUPAC Nomenclature: Alkanes
stem_template: Name the following organic compound given its SMILES: {{smiles}}
mark_scheme_template: CCCC = butane, CCCCC = pentane, CC(C)CC = 2-methylbutane, CCC(C)C = 2-methylbutane, CC(C)(C)C = 2,2-dimethylpropane [1 mark each]
hints: Find the longest carbon chain (parent chain)
Number from the end that gives substituents lowest numbers

subject: chemistry
title: Functional Group Identification
stem_template: Match each functional group to its class of compound.
mark_scheme_template: −OH = alcohol [1]; −COOH = carboxylic acid [1]; −CHO = aldehyde [1]; −CO− = ketone [1]; −NH₂ = amine [1]; −COO− = ester [1]; C=C = alkene [1]; −X = halogenoalkane [1]. [8 marks]
hints: Carboxylic acid always has COOH at end of chain
Aldehyde has CHO at end; ketone has C=O between two carbons

subject: chemistry
title: Structural Isomers
stem_template: Draw all structural isomers of {{formula}} and name each one.
mark_scheme_template: C₄H₁₀: butane (CCCC) and 2-methylpropane (CC(C)C) [2]. C₃H₇Cl: 1-chloropropane (CCCCl) and 2-chloropropane (CC(Cl)C) [2]. [marks awarded per correct named structure]
hints: Systematically vary positions of branches or functional groups
Check each isomer has the correct molecular formula

subject: chemistry
title: Calorimetry: Enthalpy Change
stem_template: In a calorimeter, \\({{m}}\\) g of water heats from \\({{T1}}\\) °C to \\({{T2}}\\) °C. Calculate the heat released. (c_water = 4.18 J g\\(^{-1}\\) K\\(^{-1}\\))
hints: q = mcΔT
Convert J to kJ by dividing by 1000

subject: chemistry
title: Hess's Law Calculation
stem_template: Given: (1) C(s) + O₂(g) → CO₂(g), \\(\\Delta H_1 = {{dH1}}\\) kJ mol\\(^{-1}\\); (2) H₂(g) + ½O₂(g) → H₂O(l), \\(\\Delta H_2 = {{dH2}}\\) kJ mol\\(^{-1}\\); (3) C₂H₆(g) + 7/2 O₂(g) → 2CO₂(g) + 3H₂O(l), \\(\\Delta H_3 = {{dH3}}\\) kJ mol\\(^{-1}\\). Calculate \\(\\Delta H_f\\) for C₂H₆(g).
hints: Write target equation and manipulate given equations to produce it
Multiplying equation × n also × n the ΔH; reversing flips the sign

subject: chemistry
title: Bond Enthalpy Calculation
stem_template: Using bond enthalpy data, calculate ΔH for: CH₄(g) + 2O₂(g) → CO₂(g) + 2H₂O(g). Bond enthalpies (kJ mol⁻¹): C−H 413, O=O 498, C=O 805, O−H 463.
mark_scheme_template: Bonds broken (endothermic): 4×C−H + 2×O=O = 4(413) + 2(498) = 1652 + 996 = 2648 kJ [2]. Bonds formed (exothermic): 2×C=O + 4×O−H = 2(805) + 4(463) = 1610 + 1852 = 3462 kJ [2]. ΔH = 2648 − 3462 = −814 kJ mol⁻¹ [1]. (Note: value differs slightly from Hess's law due to average bond enthalpies.) [5 marks]
hints: Bond breaking: endothermic (+ΔH)
Bond forming: exothermic (−ΔH)
ΔH = Σ(broken) − Σ(formed)

subject: chemistry
title: Born-Haber Cycle for NaCl
stem_template: Using the Born-Haber cycle, calculate the lattice enthalpy of NaCl given: ΔHf(NaCl) = −411 kJ mol⁻¹, atomization energy of Na = +108 kJ mol⁻¹, atomization energy of Cl₂ = +122 kJ mol⁻¹, IE₁(Na) = +496 kJ mol⁻¹, EA(Cl) = −349 kJ mol⁻¹.
mark_scheme_template: Born-Haber: ΔHf = ΔH_atom(Na) + ΔH_atom(½Cl₂) + IE₁(Na) + EA(Cl) + ΔH_lattice. −411 = +108 + 122 + 496 + (−349) + ΔH_lattice. ΔH_lattice = −411 − (108+122+496−349) = −411 − 377 = −788 kJ mol⁻¹ [4 marks]
hints: Sum all steps around the cycle must equal ΔHf
Lattice enthalpy is highly negative (exothermic formation of ions from gaseous state)

subject: chemistry
title: Enthalpy of Solution
stem_template: The lattice enthalpy of MgCl₂ is −2526 kJ mol⁻¹ and the hydration enthalpy is −2895 kJ mol⁻¹. What is the enthalpy of solution?
mark_scheme_template: ΔH_sol = −ΔH_lattice + ΔH_hydration = +2526 + (−2895) = −369 kJ mol⁻¹. Note: +ΔH_lattice to reverse lattice formation (endothermic dissolution of lattice). [2 marks]
hints: ΔH_sol = −ΔH_lattice + ΔH_hydration
Breaking the lattice is endothermic; hydration is exothermic

subject: chemistry
title: Enthalpy of Combustion Calculation
stem_template: Burning \\({{m}}\\) g of ethanol (M_r = 46 g mol\\(^{-1}\\)) raises the temperature of \\({{mw}}\\) g of water by \\({{deltaT}}\\) °C. Calculate the enthalpy of combustion per mole. (c_water = 4.18 J g\\(^{-1}\\) K\\(^{-1}\\))
mark_scheme_template: q = \\(mc\\Delta T = {{mw}} \\times 4.18 \\times {{deltaT}} = {{round(mw*4.18*deltaT,1)}}\\) J [1]. n(ethanol) = {{m}}/46 = {{round(m/46,4)}} mol [1]. \\(\\Delta H_c = -q/n = {{round(-mw*4.18*deltaT/1000*46/m,1)}}\\) kJ mol⁻¹ [1]. Negative because exothermic. [3 marks]
hints: ΔHc = −(heat gained by water) / moles of fuel
The sign is negative for an exothermic combustion

subject: chemistry
title: Fuel Cell vs Combustion Engine
stem_template: Which statement about hydrogen fuel cells is correct?
answer_template: They convert chemical energy directly to electrical energy with water as the only product
distractor_templates: They produce CO₂ and water as combustion products
They require ignition and produce heat then electricity
They store electricity like a battery that can be recharged by plugging in

options_template.text: They convert chemical energy directly to electrical energy; only by-product is water
They combust hydrogen to produce heat, which drives a turbine
They produce CO₂ and water like other fossil fuel combustion
They are secondary cells that can be recharged by an external power source

mark_scheme_template: H₂ fuel cell: H₂ oxidized at anode, O₂ reduced at cathode, producing water and electricity. No combustion, no CO₂. Efficiency higher than combustion engines. [1 mark]
hints: Fuel cells are galvanic cells that use fuel (H₂) rather than metal electrodes

subject: chemistry
title: Gibbs Free Energy: Spontaneity
stem_template: Calculate ΔG for a reaction at \\({{T}}\\) °C, given ΔH = \\({{dH}}\\) kJ mol\\(^{-1}\\) and ΔS = \\({{dS}}\\) J mol\\(^{-1}\\) K\\(^{-1}\\). Is the reaction spontaneous?
mark_scheme_template: \\(\\Delta G = \\Delta H - T\\Delta S = {{dH}} \\times 10^3 - {{T+273}} \\times {{dS}} = {{dH*1000 - (T+273)*dS}}\\) J mol⁻¹ [2]. If ΔG < 0: spontaneous; if ΔG > 0: non-spontaneous [1]. [3 marks]
hints: ΔG = ΔH − TΔS (T in Kelvin)
Must convert ΔH to J mol⁻¹ or ΔS to kJ mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ for consistent units
Spontaneous if ΔG < 0

subject: chemistry
title: Entropy Change Prediction
stem_template: Which process involves an increase in entropy?

options_template.text: AgCl(s) precipitates from solution [ions become ordered solid]

mark_scheme_template: Entropy increases when: solid → liquid/gas, liquid → gas, or number of gas moles increases. CaCO₃(s) → CaO(s) + CO₂(g): a gas is produced → ΔS > 0. [1 mark]
hints: Gases have much higher entropy than liquids or solids
Count moles of gas: increase → ΔS > 0

subject: chemistry
title: Stoichiometry: Limiting Reagent
stem_template: In the reaction 2Al + 3Cl₂ → 2AlCl₃, \\({{mAl}}\\) g of Al reacts with \\({{mCl2}}\\) g of Cl₂. Which is the limiting reagent and how many moles of AlCl₃ are produced? (M_r: Al = 27, Cl₂ = 71, AlCl₃ = 133.5)
answer_template: Compare mole ratios to determine limiting reagent
mark_scheme_template: n(Al) = {{mAl}}/27 = {{round(mAl/27,3)}} mol; n(Cl₂) = {{mCl2}}/71 = {{round(mCl2/71,3)}} mol [1]. Ratio required: Al:Cl₂ = 2:3 → for {{round(mAl/27,3)}} mol Al need {{round(mAl/27*1.5,3)}} mol Cl₂ [1]. Compare with available {{round(mCl2/71,3)}} mol Cl₂ to identify limiting reagent [1]. n(AlCl₃) = n(Al limiting) × 1 [1]. [4 marks]
hints: Find moles of each reactant
Compare actual ratio with stoichiometric ratio
Limiting reagent is the one that runs out first

subject: chemistry
title: Percentage Yield
stem_template: The theoretical yield of aspirin in a synthesis is \\({{theoretical}}\\) g. The actual yield obtained is \\({{actual}}\\) g. Calculate the percentage yield.
mark_scheme_template: \\(\\%\\text{ yield} = \\frac{\\text{actual yield}}{\\text{theoretical yield}} \\times 100 = \\frac{{{actual}}}{{{theoretical}}} \\times 100 = {{round(actual/theoretical*100,1)}}\\% \\) [1 mark]
hints: % yield = (actual/theoretical) × 100%

subject: chemistry
title: Rate of Reaction: Factors
stem_template: Increasing the temperature of a reaction primarily increases the rate by:
answer_template: Increasing the fraction of particles with energy ≥ activation energy
distractor_templates: Increasing the activation energy of the reaction
Increasing the frequency of collisions only
Decreasing the number of particles needed

options_template.text: Increasing the fraction of particles with energy ≥ Ea AND increasing collision frequency
Increasing the activation energy, making reactions more favorable
Increasing collision frequency only — no change in fraction of successful collisions

mark_scheme_template: Higher temperature: (1) more particles exceed activation energy (major effect — exponential, from Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution) AND (2) more frequent collisions (minor effect). [2 marks]
hints: Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution shifts to higher energies at higher T
Both collision frequency and fraction of successful collisions increase

subject: chemistry
title: Arrhenius Equation and Activation Energy
stem_template: The rate constant for a reaction is \\({{k1}}\\) × 10⁻³ s⁻¹ at \\({{T1}}\\) K and \\({{k2}}\\) × 10⁻³ s⁻¹ at \\({{T2}}\\) K. Use the Arrhenius equation to calculate the activation energy. (R = 8.31 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹)
mark_scheme_template: \\(\\ln\\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \\frac{E_a}{R}\\left(\\frac{1}{T_1} - \\frac{1}{T_2}\\right)\\) [1]. \\(E_a = \\frac{R \\ln(k_2/k_1)}{1/T_1 - 1/T_2}\\) [1]. Substitute values and calculate [2]. [4 marks]
hints: Arrhenius: ln(k2/k1) = Ea/R × (1/T1 − 1/T2)
Rearrange for Ea

subject: chemistry
title: Equilibrium Constant Kc
stem_template: For the reaction A(aq) + B(aq) ⇌ C(aq), at equilibrium: [A] = \\({{A}}\\) mol dm⁻³, [B] = \\({{B}}\\) mol dm⁻³, [C] = \\({{C}}\\) mol dm⁻³. Calculate Kc.
mark_scheme_template: \\(K_c = \\frac{[C]}{[A][B]} = \\frac{{{C}}}{{{A}} \\times {{B}}} = {{round(C/(A*B),2)}}\\) dm³ mol⁻¹ [2 marks]
hints: Kc = [products] / [reactants] (each raised to stoichiometric power)
Only include aqueous and gaseous species

subject: chemistry
title: Le Chatelier's Principle
stem_template: For N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), ΔH = −92 kJ mol⁻¹. Which change shifts equilibrium to the RIGHT (more NH₃)?
distractor_templates: Add an inert gas at constant volume

options_template.text: Increase pressure (fewer moles of gas on right: 4 → 2)
Increase temperature (reaction is exothermic; high T favors reverse)
Add inert gas at constant volume (no change in partial pressures)

mark_scheme_template: Increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. Left = 1+3 = 4 mol; Right = 2 mol. Equilibrium shifts RIGHT. [1 mark]
hints: Le Chatelier: equilibrium shifts to oppose the change
Pressure: shifts to side with fewer moles of gas

subject: chemistry
title: Rate Expression and Reaction Order
stem_template: From the following data for A + B → products, determine the rate expression and value of k. Experiment 1: [A]=0.10, [B]=0.10, rate=2.0×10⁻³; Experiment 2: [A]=0.20, [B]=0.10, rate=8.0×10⁻³; Experiment 3: [A]=0.10, [B]=0.30, rate=6.0×10⁻³ (all in mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹ and mol dm⁻³).
answer_template: rate = k[A]²[B]; k = 2.0 dm⁶ mol⁻² s⁻¹
mark_scheme_template: Exp 1→2: [A] doubles, rate ×4 → order w.r.t A = 2 [1]. Exp 1→3: [B] triples, rate ×3 → order w.r.t B = 1 [1]. Rate = k[A]²[B] [1]. k = rate/([A]²[B]) = 2.0×10⁻³/(0.10²×0.10) = 2.0 dm⁶ mol⁻² s⁻¹ [1]. [4 marks]
hints: Compare experiments where only one concentration changes
If doubling [A] quadruples rate → 2nd order in A

subject: chemistry
title: Half-Life of First-Order Reaction
stem_template: A first-order reaction has rate constant k = \\({{k}}\\) × 10⁻³ s⁻¹. What is the half-life?
mark_scheme_template: For first-order reaction: \\(t_{1/2} = \\frac{\\ln 2}{k} = \\frac{0.693}{{{k}} \\times 10^{-3}} = {{round(0.693/(k*1e-3),0)}}\\) s [2 marks]
hints: First-order: t½ = ln2/k = 0.693/k
Half-life is constant and independent of concentration for first-order

subject: chemistry
title: pH of Strong Acid
stem_template: Calculate the pH of a \\({{c}}\\) mol dm⁻³ solution of hydrochloric acid (assume complete dissociation).
mark_scheme_template: HCl is strong acid: [H⁺] = {{c}} mol dm⁻³. pH = −log[H⁺] = −log({{c}}) = {{round(-log10(c),2)}} [2 marks]
hints: pH = −log₁₀[H⁺]
Strong acids fully dissociate: [H⁺] = [acid]

subject: chemistry
title: pH of Weak Acid (Ka)
stem_template: Calculate the pH of a \\({{c}}\\) mol dm⁻³ solution of ethanoic acid (Ka = \\({{Ka}}\\) × 10⁻⁵ mol dm⁻³).
mark_scheme_template: Using approximation (weak acid, Ka << c): [H⁺] ≈ \\(\\sqrt{K_a c} = \\sqrt{{{Ka}} \\times 10^{-5} \\times {{c}}} = {{round((Ka*1e-5*c)**0.5,6)}}\\) mol dm⁻³ [2]. pH = −log({{round((Ka*1e-5*c)**0.5,6)}}) = {{round(-log10((Ka*1e-5*c)**0.5),2)}} [1]. [3 marks]
hints: For weak acid: [H⁺] ≈ √(Ka × c) if α << 1
pH = −log[H⁺]

subject: chemistry
title: Buffer Solution pH Calculation
stem_template: A buffer is made from \\({{nacid}}\\) mol of ethanoic acid and \\({{nsalt}}\\) mol of sodium ethanoate in 1 dm³. Calculate the pH. (Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ mol dm⁻³)
mark_scheme_template: Henderson-Hasselbalch: \\(\\text{pH} = \\text{p}K_a + \\log\\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\\) [1]. pKa = −log(1.8×10⁻⁵) = 4.74 [1]. pH = 4.74 + log({{nsalt}}/{{nacid}}) = {{round(4.74 + log10(nsalt/nacid),2)}} [2]. [4 marks]
hints: Buffer: mixture of weak acid and its conjugate base
Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([base]/[acid])

subject: chemistry
title: Oxidation State Assignment
stem_template: Assign oxidation states to all elements in: (a) Cr₂O₇²⁻ (b) KMnO₄ (c) Na₂S₂O₃
answer_template: Use rules: O = −2, H = +1, sum = overall charge
hints: Sum of oxidation states = overall charge of species
O = −2 (usually), H = +1 (in compounds), Group 1 metals = +1

subject: chemistry
title: Electrolysis: Electrode Products
stem_template: During electrolysis of aqueous copper(II) sulfate using copper electrodes, what happens at the anode?
distractor_templates: Copper is deposited on the anode

options_template.text: Oxygen gas is produced by oxidation of water
Copper is deposited at the anode (reduction)
Hydrogen gas is produced from H⁺ ions

mark_scheme_template: Active copper anode: Cu is oxidized preferentially over water → Cu dissolves into solution as Cu²⁺. This replenishes the Cu²⁺ used at the cathode (electroplating). [1 mark]
hints: Active metal electrodes dissolve preferentially at the anode
Inert electrodes (Pt, graphite): O₂ produced at anode from water

subject: chemistry
title: Electrochemical Cell: EMF Calculation
stem_template: Calculate the standard EMF of a cell made from Zn²⁺/Zn (E° = −0.76 V) and Cu²⁺/Cu (E° = +0.34 V) half-cells.
mark_scheme_template: E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode = +0.34 − (−0.76) = +1.10 V [2]. Zn is anode (oxidized, more negative E°), Cu is cathode (reduced, more positive E°) [1]. [3 marks]
hints: E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode
More negative E° → anode (oxidation)

subject: chemistry
title: Electrophilic Addition to Alkenes
stem_template: Write the mechanism for the addition of HBr to {{alkene}}. Include the curly arrow mechanism and identify the major product using Markovnikov's rule.
answer_template: Markovnikov: H adds to less substituted carbon; Br⁻ to more substituted carbocation
mark_scheme_template: Step 1: π electrons of C=C attack H of HBr (electrophile) → most stable (2° or 3°) carbocation intermediate [2]. Step 2: Br⁻ attacks carbocation [1]. Major product: Br on more substituted carbon (Markovnikov) [1]. Minor product noted [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Electrophile attacks first (H⁺ from HBr)
More stable carbocation forms at more substituted carbon
Markovnikov: halide adds to carbon with more hydrogens

subject: chemistry
title: Free Radical Substitution in Alkanes
stem_template: In the free-radical chlorination of methane, which step produces new free radicals?

options_template.text: Propagation (chain-carrying steps that produce new radicals)
Initiation (generates first radicals from Cl₂, but none of the new C-centered ones)
Elimination (not relevant to free radical mechanism)

mark_scheme_template: Propagation steps: CH₄ + Cl• → CH₃• + HCl (radical maintained). Each step regenerates a radical, allowing chain to continue. Initiation only makes Cl•; termination destroys radicals. [1 mark]
hints: Initiation: Cl₂ → 2Cl• (UV)
Propagation: radicals react but new ones form
Termination: two radicals combine

subject: chemistry
title: Nucleophilic Substitution of Halogenoalkanes
stem_template: Compare the hydrolysis of 1-bromobutane and 2-bromo-2-methylpropane with aqueous NaOH. Discuss the mechanism (SN1 vs SN2), rate dependence, and stereochemistry.
answer_template: SN2 for primary, SN1 for tertiary
mark_scheme_template: 1-Bromobutane (primary): SN2 — bimolecular, rate = k[RBr][OH⁻], backside attack → inversion of configuration [2]. 2-Bromo-2-methylpropane (tertiary): SN1 — unimolecular, rate = k[RBr], carbocation intermediate → racemization [2]. Primary C not stabilized as carbocation; tertiary forms stable 3° carbocation [1]. [5 marks]
hints: SN2: one step, backside attack, inversion (Walden inversion)
SN1: two steps, planar carbocation, racemic mixture

subject: chemistry
title: SN2 Reaction: Mechanism and Stereochemistry
stem_template: Draw the transition state for the SN2 reaction of (R)-2-bromobutane with OH⁻. Predict the product's configuration and explain why inversion occurs.
mark_scheme_template: Transition state: trigonal bipyramidal with OH and Br at apices, partially bonded [2]. Backside attack by OH⁻ on the carbon bearing Br [1]. Inversion of configuration (Walden inversion): R → S [1]. Product: (S)-butan-2-ol [1]. [5 marks]
hints: SN2: backside attack required by geometry
Transition state has 5 groups around central carbon
Configuration inverts: R → S or S → R

subject: chemistry
title: Addition Polymers vs Condensation Polymers
stem_template: Which statement correctly distinguishes addition from condensation polymerization?
answer_template: Condensation releases a small molecule (water); addition does not
distractor_templates: Addition uses monomers with two functional groups; condensation uses monomers with one
Addition always requires a catalyst; condensation does not
Addition forms branched polymers; condensation forms linear chains

options_template.text: Condensation releases a small molecule (e.g. H₂O) per bond formed; addition does not
Addition uses two different bifunctional monomers; condensation uses one monomer type
Addition always requires UV light; condensation requires heat only
Addition forms only branched polymers; condensation always linear

mark_scheme_template: Addition polymerization: alkene monomers add together, all atoms incorporated into polymer chain, no by-product [1]. Condensation polymerization: monomers with 2 functional groups react, eliminating small molecules (H₂O, HCl, etc.) per linkage formed [1]. [2 marks]
hints: Addition: C=C monomers link together
Condensation: e.g., ester linkages (polyester) or amide linkages (nylon)

subject: chemistry
title: Elimination vs Substitution: Competition
stem_template: 2-Bromopropane reacts with: (i) NaOH(aq) at room temperature, (ii) NaOH in ethanol under reflux. Identify the major products in each case and explain the factors determining the outcome.
mark_scheme_template: (i) Aqueous NaOH: OH⁻ acts as nucleophile (good in polar protic solvent), SN2 favored → propan-2-ol [2]. (ii) Ethanolic NaOH, heat: OH⁻ acts as base (E2 mechanism), removes β-H → propene [2]. Higher temperature and non-polar solvent favor elimination [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Aqueous = nucleophilic substitution; ethanolic + heat = elimination
OH⁻ can act as nucleophile or base depending on conditions

subject: chemistry
title: Condensation Polymerization: Nylon Formation
stem_template: Draw the repeating unit of nylon-6,6 formed from 1,6-diaminohexane and hexanedioic acid (adipic acid). Show the amide linkage and the small molecule eliminated.
answer_template: Repeating unit contains −CO−(CH₂)₄−CO−NH−(CH₂)₆−NH− with H₂O eliminated
mark_scheme_template: Diamine: H₂N−(CH₂)₆−NH₂ + Diacid: HOOC−(CH₂)₄−COOH [1]. Amide bond (−CO−NH−) formed [1]. H₂O eliminated per bond [1]. Repeating unit: −[NH(CH₂)₆NHCO(CH₂)₄CO]_n− [2]. [5 marks]
hints: Amide bond forms between −COOH and −NH₂
Nylon-6,6 has 6 carbons from each monomer
H₂O is the condensate

subject: chemistry
title: Number of Molecules from Moles
stem_template: Calculate the number of molecules in \\({{n}}\\) mol of water. (N_A = 6.02 x 10\\(^{23}\\) mol\\(^{-1}\\))
mark_scheme_template: N = n x N_A = {{n}} x 6.02 x 10^23 molecules [1 mark]
hints: N = n x NA

subject: chemistry
title: Interpreting a Mass Spectrum
stem_template: A mass spectrum shows peaks at m/z = 78 (100%) and m/z = 79 (6.7%). Explain what these peaks represent and estimate the number of carbon atoms.
answer_template: M+ ion at 78; M+1 peak from 13C; ~6 carbon atoms
mark_scheme_template: m/z = 78: molecular ion M+ (most abundant isotope) [1]. m/z = 79: M+1 peak from 13C isotope (1.1% per C) [1]. M+1 intensity ~6.7% → ~6 carbon atoms (6 x 1.1% ≈ 6.6%) [2]. [4 marks]
hints: m/z values correspond to fragment mass
M+1 peak: (M+1)/M% ≈ 1.1 x number of carbons

subject: chemistry
title: Real vs Ideal Gas Deviations
stem_template: Explain when real gases deviate most from ideal behavior. Describe the two van der Waals corrections.
answer_template: High P and low T; volume correction (b) and pressure correction (a/V^2)
mark_scheme_template: Deviations greatest at: high pressure (molecules close, intermolecular forces significant) and low temperature (molecules slow, near condensation) [2]. VdW: (1) pressure correction +a/V^2 for intermolecular attractions [1]; (2) volume correction -b for finite molecular volume [1]. Equation: (P + a/V^2)(V - b) = nRT [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Ideal: no volume, no intermolecular forces
High P: volume correction; low T: pressure correction

subject: chemistry
title: Lattice Energy Trends
stem_template: Arrange in order of increasing lattice energy: NaCl, MgO, CaO, NaF. Explain using Coulomb's law.
mark_scheme_template: Lattice energy proportional to q+q-/r. NaCl (1+1-, larger r) < NaF (1+1-, smaller r) < CaO (2+2-, larger r) < MgO (2+2-, smallest r). Higher charges and smaller radii give larger lattice energies. [4 marks]
hints: E proportional to q1*q2/r
Higher charges and smaller ions increase lattice energy

subject: chemistry
title: Metallic Bonding: Melting Point Trend
stem_template: Which metal has the highest melting point and why?
answer_template: Tungsten (W) — most delocalized d-electrons, strongest metallic bonding

options_template.text: Tungsten (W) — small radius, many delocalized d-electrons, very strong metallic bond (mp 3422 degrees C)
Iron (Fe) — common transition metal, moderate melting point
Copper (Cu) — good conductor but mp only 1085 degrees C
Sodium (Na) — alkali metal, soft and low melting point (98 degrees C)

mark_scheme_template: W has highest mp: small atomic radius + high nuclear charge + multiple delocalized d-electrons per atom = very strong metallic bonds. [2 marks]
hints: More delocalized electrons per atom = stronger metallic bond
Transition metals generally have higher melting points

subject: chemistry
title: Identifying Intermolecular Forces in Common Molecules
stem_template: Identify all intermolecular forces in: (a) CH4 (b) HCl (c) C2H5OH (d) CH3COCH3
answer_template: London in all; dipole-dipole for polar; H-bond for O-H groups
mark_scheme_template: (a) CH4: London dispersion forces only [1]. (b) HCl: London + permanent dipole-dipole (polar H-Cl, no H-bond) [1]. (c) Ethanol: London + dipole-dipole + hydrogen bonding (O-H) [1]. (d) Propanone: London + dipole-dipole (C=O polar, no O-H/N-H) [1]. [4 marks]
hints: All molecules have London forces
H-bonds need N-H, O-H, or F-H groups

subject: chemistry
title: Atomic Radius Periodic Trend
stem_template: Which statement about atomic radius trends is correct?
answer_template: Decreases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge with constant shielding

options_template.text: Decreases across a period: nuclear charge increases, shielding constant, electrons pulled closer
Increases across a period due to more electron-electron repulsion
Decreases down a group due to increasing nuclear charge
Is constant within a period as the electron count increases

mark_scheme_template: Across a period: Z increases, shielding roughly constant (same shell) → effective nuclear charge increases → electrons pulled closer → smaller radius. Down a group: new shell added each period → larger radius. [2 marks]
hints: Effective nuclear charge = Z - shielding
More protons, same shielding → electrons pulled in

subject: chemistry
title: IUPAC Name for a Secondary Alcohol
stem_template: What is the IUPAC name for CH3CH(OH)CH2CH3?
mark_scheme_template: 4-carbon chain (butane), OH on carbon 2 → butan-2-ol. Number from end giving OH lowest number. [1 mark]
hints: Find longest chain containing OH
Give OH lowest possible number

subject: chemistry
title: Sign Convention for Enthalpy Change
stem_template: For a reaction with DeltaH = -120 kJ mol^-1, which statement is correct?
answer_template: Exothermic; products have lower enthalpy than reactants; surroundings warm up
distractor_templates: Delta H negative means entropy increases
Products have higher energy than reactants

options_template.text: Exothermic; products at lower enthalpy than reactants; surroundings warm up
Endothermic; energy is absorbed; surroundings cool down
Negative DeltaH means entropy is decreasing
Products have higher enthalpy than reactants

mark_scheme_template: DeltaH < 0 → exothermic. Products are more stable (lower H). Energy released to surroundings → temperature increases. [1 mark]
hints: Negative DeltaH = exothermic = energy released

subject: chemistry
title: Energy Density Comparison: H2 vs CH4
stem_template: Compare the energy density (kJ per g) of hydrogen (DeltaHc = -286 kJ/mol, M=2) and methane (DeltaHc = -890 kJ/mol, M=16). Discuss practical advantages and disadvantages of hydrogen as a fuel.
answer_template: H2: 143 kJ/g; CH4: 55.6 kJ/g; H2 has higher energy density per gram
mark_scheme_template: H2: 286/2 = 143 kJ/g [1]. CH4: 890/16 = 55.6 kJ/g [1]. H2 higher by mass [1]. Advantages: zero CO2; higher energy per gram; fuel cell use [1]. Disadvantages: very low density by volume; storage difficult; currently produced from fossil fuels; flammable [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Energy density = |DeltaHc| / molar mass
Consider both mass and volumetric density

subject: chemistry
title: Temperature for Spontaneity from DeltaH and DeltaS
stem_template: A reaction has DeltaH = +200 kJ/mol and DeltaS = +500 J/(mol K). At what temperature does it become spontaneous?
mark_scheme_template: Spontaneous when DeltaG < 0. DeltaG = DeltaH - TDeltaS < 0. TDeltaS > DeltaH. T > DeltaH/DeltaS = 200000/500 = 400 K [2]. Above 400 K: entropy term drives reaction forward [1]. DeltaS > 0: products are more disordered (e.g. gas produced) [1]. [4 marks]
hints: Set DeltaG = 0 to find crossover T
T_crossover = DeltaH/DeltaS (consistent units: J throughout)

subject: chemistry
title: Atom Economy: Fermentation of Glucose
stem_template: Calculate the atom economy for: C6H12O6 → 2C2H5OH + 2CO2. (Mr: C2H5OH=46, CO2=44, C6H12O6=180)
mark_scheme_template: Atom economy = (sum Mr of desired products / sum Mr of all products) x 100% [1]. Desired: 2 x 46 = 92. All: 92 + 2x44 = 180. Atom economy = 92/180 x 100 = 51.1% [2]. [3 marks]
hints: Atom economy = (mass of desired products / mass of all products) x 100%
Higher atom economy = greener chemistry

subject: chemistry
title: How Catalysts Increase Reaction Rate
stem_template: How does a catalyst increase the rate of a chemical reaction?
answer_template: Provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy

options_template.text: Provides an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy Ea
Increases the temperature, giving particles more kinetic energy
Shifts the equilibrium position to favor products

mark_scheme_template: Catalyst: alternative mechanism with lower Ea → larger fraction of molecules exceed Ea → faster rate. Catalyst unchanged, equilibrium position unaffected. [2 marks]
hints: Catalyst lowers Ea
Catalyst does not change equilibrium constant Kc

subject: chemistry
title: Calculating Kp for Haber Process
stem_template: For N2 + 3H2 ⇌ 2NH3 at 400°C: P(N2)=20 atm, P(H2)=60 atm, P(NH3)=10 atm. Calculate Kp.
hints: Kp uses partial pressures raised to stoichiometric powers
Units from exponents: delta_n(gas) = 2-4 = -2

subject: chemistry
title: Reaction Mechanism and Rate-Determining Step
stem_template: The reaction 2NO + O2 → 2NO2 has rate = k[NO]^2[O2]. Propose a two-step mechanism consistent with this rate law.
mark_scheme_template: Step 1 (RDS/slow): 2NO → N2O2 [1]. Step 2 (fast): N2O2 + O2 → 2NO2 [1]. Rate from RDS: rate = k[NO]^2. N2O2 equilibrium: [N2O2] = K[NO]^2. Rate of step 2 = k2[N2O2][O2] = k2K[NO]^2[O2] consistent with observed rate law [2]. [4 marks]
hints: RDS determines rate law
Intermediates must not appear in final rate expression

subject: chemistry
title: Conjugate Acid of a Base
stem_template: In NH3 + H2O ⇌ NH4+ + OH−, what is the conjugate acid of NH3?

options_template.text: NH\\(_4^+\\) (NH3 gains H+ to form its conjugate acid)
NH\\(_2^-\\) (this is the conjugate base of NH3)
OH\\(^-\\) (conjugate base of H2O, not NH3)
H\\(_2\\)O (the Bronsted-Lowry acid in this reaction)

mark_scheme_template: NH3 is the base: accepts H+ from H2O. Conjugate acid = NH3 + H+ = NH4+. [1 mark]
hints: Conjugate acid = base + H+
Base accepts H+; conjugate acid is the result

subject: chemistry
title: Titration Curve: Weak Acid vs Strong Base
stem_template: Sketch the pH curve for adding 0.1 mol dm^-3 NaOH to 25 cm^3 of 0.1 mol dm^-3 ethanoic acid. (a) Identify the equivalence point. (b) Suggest a suitable indicator and explain.
answer_template: Equivalence at 25 cm3 NaOH; pH > 7; phenolphthalein
mark_scheme_template: (a) Equivalence at 25 cm3 NaOH. Solution at equivalence = sodium ethanoate (weak base) → pH > 7 (approx 8.7) [2]. (b) Phenolphthalein (range 8.3-10.0) is suitable: changes colour at equivalence point. Methyl orange (3.1-4.4) not suitable: changes before equivalence [2]. Buffer region around 12.5 cm3, pH ≈ pKa = 4.74 [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Weak acid + strong base: equivalence pH > 7
Indicator range must encompass the equivalence point

subject: chemistry
title: Electrons Transferred in MnO4- Reduction
stem_template: In acidic solution, MnO4- is reduced to Mn2+. How many electrons are transferred per MnO4- ion?

options_template.text: 7 (equals the oxidation state of Mn in MnO4-)

mark_scheme_template: Mn in MnO4-: +7. Mn in Mn2+: +2. Change = 5. Electrons transferred = 5 per MnO4-. [1 mark]
hints: Electrons transferred = magnitude of change in oxidation state

subject: chemistry
title: Product of Bromine Addition to Propene
stem_template: What is the product when propene (CH3CH=CH2) reacts with Br2?
mark_scheme_template: Electrophilic addition: Br2 adds across C=C. Both C of double bond each bond to Br → 1,2-dibromopropane. [1 mark]
hints: Both carbons of C=C each acquire one Br atom

subject: chemistry
title: Shapes of Atomic Orbitals
stem_template: What is the shape of a p orbital?

options_template.text: Dumbbell-shaped (two lobes on opposite sides of nucleus)

mark_scheme_template: p orbitals have two lobes (dumbbell shape) oriented along one of three axes (px, py, pz). Each p orbital has a nodal plane through the nucleus. [1 mark]
hints: s: sphere, p: dumbbell, d: various complex shapes

subject: chemistry
title: Avogadro's Number Application
stem_template: How many atoms are in 2.5 mol of iron?

subject: chemistry
title: Bond Angle in Water Molecule
stem_template: The H-O-H bond angle in water is approximately 104.5°. Why is this less than the tetrahedral angle of 109.5°?
answer_template: Two lone pairs on O repel bonding pairs, reducing bond angle
distractor_templates: H atoms are large and crowd each other
O-H bonds are longer than in a perfect tetrahedron
The molecule is linear, not tetrahedral

options_template.text: Two lone pairs on O exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs, compressing H-O-H angle
H atoms are large and crowd each other at smaller angles
The O-H bonds are shorter than in ideal tetrahedron
The water molecule is linear with 180 degrees

mark_scheme_template: VSEPR: lone pair-lone pair repulsion > lone pair-bond pair > bond pair-bond pair. Water has 2 LP + 2 BP: LPs compress the H-O-H angle from 109.5 to 104.5 degrees. [2 marks]
hints: Lone pairs take more space than bonding pairs
More lone pairs → more compression of bond angles

subject: chemistry
title: Enthalpy of Hydration and Enthalpy of Solution
stem_template: Using lattice enthalpy and hydration enthalpy, explain why some ionic compounds dissolve exothermically while others dissolve endothermically.
answer_template: DeltaH_sol = -DeltaH_lattice + DeltaH_hydration; sign depends on relative magnitudes
mark_scheme_template: DeltaH_sol = -LE + DeltaH_hyd(+ + -) [1]. If |DeltaH_hyd| > LE: exothermic dissolution (e.g. NaOH) [1]. If LE > |DeltaH_hyd|: endothermic dissolution (e.g. NaCl, NH4NO3) [1]. Lattice enthalpy depends on charge and radius of ions; hydration depends on charge density of each ion [1]. [4 marks]
hints: DeltaH_sol = endothermic lattice breaking + exothermic hydration
Net sign depends on which is larger

subject: chemistry
title: Concentration Calculation
stem_template: \\({{n}}\\) mol of NaCl is dissolved in \\({{V}}\\) mL of water. What is the concentration in mol dm\\(^{-3}\\)?
mark_scheme_template: c = n/V. Convert V to dm3: {{V}} mL = {{V/1000}} dm3. c = {{n}} / {{V/1000}} = {{round(n/(V/1000),3)}} mol dm-3 [1 mark]
hints: c = n / V (V in dm3)

subject: chemistry
title: Nucleophilic Addition to Aldehydes
stem_template: Draw the mechanism for the nucleophilic addition of HCN to ethanal (CH3CHO). Name the product and state its significance.
mark_scheme_template: CN- attacks electrophilic carbonyl carbon (nucleophile) [1]. C=O bond breaks heterolytically, O becomes O- [1]. H+ (from HCN) protonates O- to give OH group [1]. Product: 2-hydroxypropanenitrile (CH3CH(OH)CN) [1]. Significance: adds one carbon to chain (chain extension); can be hydrolyzed to amino acids [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Carbonyl C is electrophilic (delta+)
CN- is the nucleophile (negative C in C≡N)
Product is a cyanohydrin: HO-CR-CN

subject: chemistry
title: Diagonal Relationship: Li and Mg
stem_template: Describe the diagonal relationship between lithium and magnesium, giving three properties they share that differ from their respective group members.
answer_template: Both form stable oxides (not peroxides), carbonates that decompose on heating, hard lithium salts
mark_scheme_template: Diagonal relationship: Li (Group 1) resembles Mg (Group 2) in diagonal position. [1 mark for definition]. Shared properties: (1) Li2CO3 and MgCO3 both decompose on heating (most Group 1 carbonates do not) [1]; (2) Both form normal oxides (Li2O, MgO) not peroxides or superoxides [1]; (3) Both nitrides form on heating in N2 (Li3N, Mg3N2) [1]; (4) Similar small ionic radius and charge density [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Diagonal relationship results from similar charge density (charge/radius ratio)
Compare properties across vs down periodic table

subject: chemistry
title: Hybridization and Molecular Geometry
stem_template: For each molecule, identify the hybridization of the central atom and the molecular geometry: (a) BF3 (b) SF6 (c) PCl5.
answer_template: BF3: sp2 trigonal planar; SF6: sp3d2 octahedral; PCl5: sp3d trigonal bipyramidal
mark_scheme_template: (a) BF3: B has 3 bonding groups, 0 lone pairs → sp2 hybridization, trigonal planar, bond angle 120° [2]. (b) SF6: S has 6 bonding groups → sp3d2, octahedral, bond angle 90° [2]. (c) PCl5: P has 5 bonding groups → sp3d, trigonal bipyramidal, axial bonds 180°, equatorial 120° [2]. [6 marks]
hints: Count electron domains (bonding + lone pairs)
sp=2, sp2=3, sp3=4, sp3d=5, sp3d2=6 electron domains

subject: chemistry
title: Constructing Half-Equations and Full Redox Equation
stem_template: Construct the overall ionic equation for the reaction of Fe2+ with MnO4- in acidic solution (Fe2+ → Fe3+; MnO4- → Mn2+).
mark_scheme_template: Oxidation half: Fe2+ → Fe3+ + e- (x5) [1]. Reduction half: MnO4- + 8H+ + 5e- → Mn2+ + 4H2O [2]. Multiply oxidation half by 5 to balance electrons [1]. Overall: 5Fe2+ + MnO4- + 8H+ → 5Fe3+ + Mn2+ + 4H2O [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Balance each half-equation: atoms first, then charge using H+ and H2O in acidic solution
Multiply to equalize electron counts

subject: chemistry
title: Isotopes: Same and Different Properties
stem_template: Which property is the SAME for isotopes of the same element?

options_template.text: Chemical properties (isotopes have identical electron configurations and bonding)
Mass number (isotopes have different mass numbers by definition)
Number of neutrons (different for each isotope)

mark_scheme_template: Isotopes: same atomic number Z (same protons, same electrons) → same chemical properties. Different mass numbers (different neutron counts) → different physical properties (density, melting point) and nuclear stability. [1 mark]
hints: Isotopes: same Z, different A
Chemistry depends on electrons, not neutrons

subject: chemistry
title: Standard Enthalpy of Combustion Definition
stem_template: Which is the correct definition of standard enthalpy of combustion?
answer_template: Enthalpy change when 1 mol of substance burns completely in O2 under standard conditions
distractor_templates: Enthalpy change when any amount burns
Enthalpy change when 1 mol reacts with water
Enthalpy change for breaking all bonds in 1 mol

options_template.text: Enthalpy change when 1 mol burns completely in excess O2, all reactants and products in standard state
Enthalpy change when any amount of substance burns in air
Enthalpy change when 1 mol of substance dissolves in water
Enthalpy change for breaking all bonds in 1 mol of substance

mark_scheme_template: Standard conditions: 298 K, 100 kPa. Exactly 1 mol of substance. Complete combustion in excess O2. All reactants and products in standard state. [2 marks]
hints: Standard: 298 K, 100 kPa
Must specify 1 mole of FUEL burned

subject: chemistry
title: Maxwell-Boltzmann Distribution and Catalyst
stem_template: Sketch the Maxwell-Boltzmann energy distribution for gas particles at temperature T. On the same diagram, show the effect of: (a) increasing temperature to 2T (b) adding a catalyst. Explain how each increases the rate.
answer_template: Broader curve at higher T; same curve but lower Ea line for catalyst
mark_scheme_template: Initial curve: right-skewed, bell-shaped, starting at origin, no maximum x-axis [1]. Higher T (2T): broader curve, shifted right, lower peak, same area, more particles beyond Ea [1]. Ea shown as vertical line; area to right of Ea = fraction with sufficient energy [1]. Catalyst: same distribution curve (T unchanged), but Ea line moves LEFT (lower Ea) → larger shaded area → greater fraction with E > Ea [1]. Higher T increases both collision frequency and fraction succeeding; catalyst only increases fraction succeeding [1]. [5 marks]
hints: Maxwell-Boltzmann: x-axis = energy, y-axis = number of particles
Area to right of Ea = particles that can react
Catalyst shifts Ea, not the distribution

subject: chemistry
title: E1 vs E2 Elimination
stem_template: Which conditions favour an E2 elimination mechanism over E1?
answer_template: Strong base (like NaOH in ethanol) and primary halogenoalkane

options_template.text: Strong base (NaOH/ethanol) and primary or secondary halogenoalkane with heat
Weak base and tertiary halogenoalkane (favors E1/SN1)
Aqueous polar solvent at room temperature (favors SN2 or SN1)
Strong acid catalyst (not relevant to E1/E2 distinction)

mark_scheme_template: E2: bimolecular, requires strong base. Rate = k[RX][base]. Anti-periplanar geometry required. Favored by primary/secondary alkyl halides with strong bulky base. E1: unimolecular, slow formation of carbocation, favored by tertiary alkyl halides, polar solvents, weak/no base. [2 marks]
hints: E2: rate = k[substrate][base], needs strong base
E1: rate = k[substrate] only, forms carbocation

subject: chemistry
title: Periodic Table Trends
stem_template: Which of the following trends across Period 3 (left to right) are CORRECT? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: The number of protons increases by 1 each element

mark_scheme_template: Left→right across Period 3: atomic radius decreases (more protons attract electrons closer); IE generally increases; proton number increases by 1. FALSE: electronegativity increases; metallic character decreases.
hints: More protons = stronger nuclear attraction = smaller radius.
Metallic character decreases left to right.

subject: chemistry
title: Organic Functional Groups
stem_template: Which of the following functional groups are present in an amino acid? Select ALL that apply.
mark_scheme_template: Amino acids contain: —NH₂ (amino), —COOH (carboxyl), and an α-carbon. The —OH in COOH is part of the carboxyl, not a separate hydroxyl. No aldehyde group in standard amino acids.
hints: General amino acid structure: H₂N—CH(R)—COOH.
The α-carbon is bonded to both —NH₂ and —COOH.

subject: chemistry
title: Equilibrium Principles
stem_template: For an exothermic reversible reaction in equilibrium, which changes will shift the equilibrium to the RIGHT? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: Increasing pressure (if gases are present and products side has fewer moles of gas)

mark_scheme_template: Shifts RIGHT: lower T (exothermic favoured); higher P when products have fewer gas moles; adding reactants; removing products. Catalyst: speeds equilibrium, doesn't shift position.
hints: Le Chatelier: system opposes the change.
Catalyst: equal effect on forward and reverse.

subject: chemistry
title: Empirical Formula Determination Steps
stem_template: Arrange the following steps for finding an empirical formula from percentage composition in the CORRECT ORDER.
items: Divide each mole value by the smallest mole value
Write the empirical formula using the resulting ratios
Assume 100 g sample; convert % to mass in grams
Divide each mass by the element's molar mass to get moles
mark_scheme_template: Correct order: (1) assume 100g sample; (2) convert mass to moles (÷ molar mass); (3) divide by smallest mole value; (4) write empirical formula.
hints: Start with 100g so % = grams directly.
The final ratio gives the subscripts.
